symptoms: VM CPU usage is higher than threshold
possible_causes: Guest OS application consumes high CPU
diagnostic_steps: Collect VM CPU usage, CPU Ready and Co-Stop metrics
Collect host CPU usage and top VM CPU consumers
Check recent vMotion and DRS events

decision_tree.conclusion: Likely host CPU contention or vCPU over-provisioning

remediation: Identify and validate high CPU process inside guest OS
Migrate VM to a less busy host after approval
Review vCPU sizing and cluster capacity

symptoms: Host memory usage is higher than threshold
Active memory may not be high
possible_causes: Configured memory is high but active memory is low
diagnostic_steps: Collect host consumed memory, active memory, balloon and swap metrics
Collect VM configured memory and active memory
Check VM reservations and recent DRS events

decision_tree.conclusion: Likely high allocated memory rather than real pressure

remediation: Do not expand capacity before verifying active memory and ballooning
Review VM memory right-sizing
Migrate selected VMs if host memory pressure is confirmed

symptoms: HA admission control cannot satisfy failover policy
Cluster does not have enough failover resources
possible_causes: Failover level is configured too high
diagnostic_steps: Collect HA admission control configuration
Collect cluster host count and host state
Collect VM CPU and memory reservations

decision_tree.conclusion: Insufficient physical hosts for configured HA policy
VM reservations reduce HA failover capacity

remediation: Add host capacity or recover unavailable hosts
Review HA failover policy
Review unnecessary VM reservations

diagnostic_steps: Collect source and destination VMkernel configuration
Validate vMotion network connectivity
Check EVC and CPU compatibility

decision_tree.conclusion: Likely vMotion VMkernel or network configuration issue

remediation: Fix vMotion VMkernel network configuration
Align VLAN, subnet and MTU settings
Retry vMotion after approval

diagnostic_steps: Collect datastore usage and free space
Collect VM snapshot tree and delta disk size
Check recent backup and snapshot tasks

decision_tree.conclusion: Likely datastore capacity growth without snapshot root cause

decision_tree.condition: snapshot delta size is large and backup job failed recently

remediation: Do not delete VMDK files manually
Confirm backup job status before consolidation
Run snapshot consolidation after approval

source.title: OpsPilot VMware Datastore and Snapshot Runbook

symptoms: VM red status is an aggregate health signal and must be expanded into child alarms, config issues, dependency state, events, and metrics
possible_causes: Active VM-level triggered alarm such as CPU, memory, disk latency, VMware Tools, heartbeat, snapshot, or consolidation issue
Storage, host, cluster, or network dependency reports non-green status
VM connection state is inaccessible, orphaned, or invalid due to inventory or storage accessibility
VM configIssue indicates missing backing, unreadable VMX, device issue, or snapshot chain problem
Stale or inherited alarm state remains after the underlying condition cleared
diagnostic_steps: Confirm VM identity, power state, connection state, overallStatus, guest heartbeat, VMware Tools state, host, cluster, datastore, and network membership
Collect VM triggeredAlarmState with alarm name, status, time, acknowledgement, and event key
Collect VM configIssue details and recent VM events/tasks for the last 2-4 hours
Collect host, datastore, and network dependency status plus blast radius for VMs sharing the same host or datastore
Collect VM and dependency performance metrics before recommending any write or recovery action

decision_tree.condition: active red or yellow triggeredAlarmState exists
decision_tree.conclusion: Prioritize the child alarm and recall matching VMware golden knowledge for CPU, memory, HA, vMotion, storage, snapshot, network, tools, or heartbeat
Likely storage accessibility or vCenter inventory consistency issue
Likely VM configuration, device backing, VMX, or snapshot chain issue
Likely dependency object caused the VM aggregate red state
Likely guest OS hang, VMware Tools issue, or VM monitoring condition

decision_tree.condition: no active alarm, config issue, dependency issue, or current metric anomaly is present
decision_tree.conclusion: Treat as possible stale or inherited alarm state and require alarm history before high-confidence RCA

remediation: Do not conclude that the VM itself is failed from overallStatus alone; expand the aggregate status first
Collect missing critical evidence and mark RCA as insufficient_evidence when triggered alarms or config issues are unavailable
Use child-alarm-specific knowledge for final diagnosis once the red source is identified
Keep migration, restart, power, snapshot consolidation, and Storage vMotion behind approval
review_notes: Generated from internal attachment vm overall red.md; overallStatus red is an aggregate signal and requires evidence expansion before RCA.

symptoms: ESXi host is disconnected or not responding in vCenter
vCenter cannot communicate with ESXi host
DVS may be out of sync
diagnostic_steps: Collect host connection events
Check management network reachability
Collect hostd and vpxa logs

decision_tree.conclusion: Prioritize vCenter, DNS, or management network issue

decision_tree.condition: single host disconnected and hostd/vpxa errors exist
decision_tree.conclusion: Likely host management agent or network issue

remediation: Do not restart ESXi directly
Validate management network and DNS first
Restart management agents only after approval
